__verbose.prompt:
<|start|>system<|message|>You are ChatGPT, a large language model trained by OpenAI.
Knowledge cutoff: 2024-06
Current date: 2026-04-04

Reasoning: medium

# Valid channels: analysis, commentary, final. Channel must be included for every message.
Calls to these tools must go to the commentary channel: 'functions'.<|end|><|start|>developer<|message|># Instructions

You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers using code comments.
- NEVER change **unrelated code and comments.**
- Never add new comments to the end of a line.
- Never add trivial comments.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer readable code over of comments.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

For tool use, never modify files outside of the current working directory unless the user requested it:
~/repos/github/g0t4/mcp-server-commands

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`


## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Include "Co-Authored-By: gptoss120b"


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.


## Use `apply_patch` to edit files.

Your patch language is a stripped‑down, file‑oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply. You can think of it as a high‑level envelope:

*** Begin Patch
[ one or more file sections ]
*** End Patch

Within that envelope, you get a sequence of file operations.
You MUST include a header to specify the action you are taking.
Each operation starts with one of three headers:

*** Add File: <path> - create a new file. Every following line is a + line (the initial contents).
*** Delete File: <path> - remove an existing file. Nothing follows.
*** Update File: <path> - patch an existing file in place (optionally with a rename).

If you want to rename the file too, immediately follow with:
*** Move to: <new path>

Then one or more “hunks”, each introduced by @@ (optionally followed by a hunk header).
Within a hunk each line starts with:

- for inserted text,
* for removed text, or
  space ( ) for context.
  At the end of a truncated hunk you can emit *** End of File.

Here is the grammar:

Patch := Begin { FileOp } End
Begin := "*** Begin Patch" NEWLINE
End := "*** End Patch" NEWLINE
FileOp := AddFile | DeleteFile | UpdateFile
AddFile := "*** Add File: " path NEWLINE { "+" line NEWLINE }
DeleteFile := "*** Delete File: " path NEWLINE
UpdateFile := "*** Update File: " path NEWLINE [ MoveTo ] { Hunk }
MoveTo := "*** Move to: " newPath NEWLINE
Hunk := "@@" [ header ] NEWLINE { HunkLine } [ "*** End of File" NEWLINE ]
HunkLine := (" " | "-" | "+") text NEWLINE

A full patch can combine several operations:

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: hello.txt
+Hello world
*** Update File: src/app.py
*** Move to: src/main.py
@@ def greet():
-print("Hi")
+print("Hello, world!")
*** Delete File: obsolete.txt
*** End Patch

It is important to remember:

- You must include a header with your intended action (Add/Delete/Update)
- You must prefix new lines with `+` even when creating a new file


Tips on a failure:

- You only need enough context for a unique match!
- The line(s) you replace are context too; if they’re unique, no additional context is needed.
- Split bigger patches into smaller patches, i.e. if editing two functions, try each separately!
- Watch out for whitespace differences, i.e. blank lines

Please be curteous:

- NEVER leave comments about removed code, just get rid of it. This code lives in a git repo.
- Check your work! Especially after a failure.

### More Examples

Here's a patch file to change the `win` property's type:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@ @dataclass
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
-    gap_rms: TimeSeries
-    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
+    gap_rms: TimeSeries
+    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
*** End Patch
```

Which can be simplified to:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

Or, this could work if the `win` property is unique:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-    win: GapWindow
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

namespace functions {

// Run a process on this darwin machine
type run_process = (_: {
// Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.
argv?: string[],
// Optional to set working directory
cwd?: string,
// Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms
timeout_ms?: number,
// Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.
command_line?: string,
// Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.
stdin?: string,
}) => any;

// Patch a file
type apply_patch = (_: {
// file changes in custom diff format
patch: string,
}) => any;

// Retrieval tool (the R in RAG) for code and documents in the current workspace. Uses a vector store with embeddings of the entire codebase. And a re-ranker for sorting results.
type semantic_grep = (_: {
// number of embeddings matches to consider for reranking
embed_top_k?: number,
// query text, what you are looking for
query: string,
// number of matches to return (post reranking)
top_k?: number,
// limit matches to a vim compatible filetype. default includes all filetypes
filetype?: string,
// instructions for the query, explain the type of query
instruct?: string,
}) => any;

       function spawn_wrapped(
    command: string,
    args: string[],
    runProcessArgs: RunProcessArgs,
): SpawnPromise {
    const startTime = performance.now();

    const options: ObjectEncodingOptions & SpawnOptions = {
        // spawn options: https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#child_processspawncommand-args-options
        encoding: "utf8"
    };
    if (runProcessArgs?.cwd) {
        options.cwd = String(runProcessArgs.cwd);
    }
    const stdin = runProcessArgs?.stdin ? String(runProcessArgs.stdin) : undefined;

    const logWithElapsedTime = (msg: string, ...rest: any[]) => {
        if (!is_verbose) return;

        const elapsed = ((performance.now() - startTime) / 1000).toFixed(3);
        verbose_log(`[${elapsed}s] ${msg}`, ...rest, command, args);
    };

    let child_pid;
    const promise: SpawnPromise = new Promise<CallToolResult>((resolve, reject) => {
        if (!stdin) {
            // PRN windowsHide on Windows, signal, killSignal
            // FYI spawn_options.stdio => default is perfect ['pipe', 'pipe', 'pipe'] 
            //     order: [STDIN, STDOUT, STDERR]
            //     https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#optionsstdio 
            //     'ignore' attaches /dev/null
            //     do not set 'inherit' (causes ripgrep to see STDIN socket and search it, thus hanging)
            options.stdio = ['ignore', 'pipe', 'pipe'];
        }

        // remove timeout on spawn options (if set) so the built‑in spawn timeout does not interfere
        delete (options as any).timeout;

        // Use a detached child so we can kill the entire process group.
        options.detached = true;

        let settled = false;
        const settle = (result: SpawnResult, isError: boolean) => {
            if (settled) return;
            settled = true;
            if (timer) clearTimeout(timer);
            if (isError) {
                resolve(resultFor(result));
            } else {
                resolve(resultFor(result));
            }
        };

        const child = spawn(command, args, options);
        logWithElapsedTime(`START SPAWN child.pid: ${child.pid}`);
        child_pid = child.pid;

        let stdout = "";
        let stderr = "";

        if (child.stdin && stdin) {
            child.stdin.write(stdin);
            child.stdin.end();
        }

        if (child.stdout) {
            child.stdout.on("data", (chunk: Buffer | string) => {
                stdout += chunk.toString();
            });
        }

        if (child.stderr) {
            child.stderr.on("data", (chunk: Buffer | string) => {
                stderr += chunk.toString();
            });
        }

        child.on("exit", (code: number | null, signal: NodeJS.Signals | null) => {
            // child process streams MAY still be open when EXIT is emitted (use close if need to ensure they're closed)
            // "close" will come after "exit" once process is terminated + streams are closed
            // so for now use "close" to determine if process was terminated too, that way you can access STDOUT/STDERR reliably for returning full output to agent
            logWithElapsedTime("EXIT", { code, signal });
        });
        child.on("spawn", () => {
            // emitted after child process starts successfully
            // if child doesn't start, error emitted instead
            // emitted BEFORE any data received via stdout/stderr
            logWithElapsedTime("SPAWN")
        });

        // Timeout handling – kill the whole process group after the supplied timeout.
        let timer: NodeJS.Timeout | null = null;

        let timeoutMs = Number(runProcessArgs?.timeout_ms);
        if (Number.isNaN(timeoutMs)) {
            timeoutMs = 30_000;
        }
        timer = setTimeout(() => {
            if (process.platform !== "win32") {
                if (child.pid) { try { process.kill(-child.pid, "SIGTERM"); } catch (_) {} }
            } else {
                child.kill("SIGTERM");
            }
            const killTimeout = setTimeout(() => {
                if (process.platform !== "win32") {
                    if (child.pid) { try { process.kill(-child.pid, "SIGKILL"); } catch (_) {} }
                } else {
                    child.kill("SIGKILL");
                }
            }, 2000);
            const clearKill = () => clearTimeout(killTimeout);
            child.once("exit", clearKill);
            child.once("close", clearKill);
        }, timeoutMs);

        child.on("error", (err: Error) => {
            logWithElapsedTime("ERROR");
            // ChildProcess 'error' docs: https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#event-error
            // error running process
            // IIUC not just b/c of command failed w/ non-zero exit code
            const result: SpawnFailure = {
                stdout,
                stderr,
                //
                // one of these will always be non-null
                code: (err as any).code, // set if process exited, else null
                signal: (err as any).signal, // set if process was terminated by signal, else null
                //
                message: err.message,
                // ? killed: (err as any).killed
            };
            logWithElapsedTime("ERROR_RESULT", result);
            settle(result, true);
        });

        child.on("close", (code: number | null, signal: NodeJS.Signals | null) => {
            logWithElapsedTime("CLOSE");
            // ChildProcess 'close' docs: https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#event-close
            //   'close' is after child process ends AND stdio streams are closed
            //   - after 'exit' or 'error'

            //   either code is set, or signal, but NOT BOTH
            //   signal if process killed
            // FYI close does not mean code==0
            const result: SpawnResult = {
                stdout,
                stderr,
                //
                code: code ?? undefined,
                signal: signal ?? undefined,
                //
            };
            logWithElapsedTime("CLOSE_RESULT", result);
            settle(result, false);
        });
    });
    // FYI later (when needed) I can map this onto the promise that comes back from runProcess too (and tie into that unit test I have that needs pid to terminate it)
    // Resolve the underlying spawn result, then map to CallToolResult including PID.
    promise.pid = child_pid;
    return promise
}

   options.cwd = String(runProcessArgs.cwd);\n    }\n    const stdin = runProcessArgs?.stdin ? String(runProcessArgs.stdin) : undefined;\n\n    const logWithElapsedTime = (msg: string, ...rest: any[]) => {\n        if (!is_verbose) return;\n\n        const elapsed = ((performance.now() - startTime) / 1000).toFixed(3);\n        verbose_log(`[${elapsed}s] ${msg}`, ...rest, command, args);\n    };\n\n    let child_pid;\n    const promise: SpawnPromise = new Promise<CallToolResult>((resolve, reject) => {\n        if (!stdin) {\n            // PRN windowsHide on Windows, signal, killSignal\n            // FYI spawn_options.stdio => default is perfect ['pipe', 'pipe', 'pipe'] \n            //     order: [STDIN, STDOUT, STDERR]\n            //     https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#optionsstdio \n            //     'ignore' attaches /dev/null\n            //     do not set 'inherit' (causes ripgrep to see STDIN socket and search it, thus hanging)\n            options.stdio = ['ignore', 'pipe', 'pipe'];\n        }\n\n        // remove timeout on spawn options (if set) so the built‑in spawn timeout does not interfere\n        delete (options as any).timeout;\n\n        // Use a detached child so we can kill the entire process group.\n        options.detached = true;\n\n        let settled = false;\n        const settle = (result: SpawnResult, isError: boolean) => {\n            if (settled) return;\n            settled = true;\n            if (timer) clearTimeout(timer);\n            if (isError) {\n                resolve(resultFor(result));\n            } else {\n                resolve(resultFor(result));\n            }\n        };\n\n        const child = spawn(command, args, options);\n        logWithElapsedTime(`START SPAWN child.pid: ${child.pid}`);\n        child_pid = child.pid;\n\n        let stdout = \"\";\n        let stderr = \"\";\n\n        if (child.stdin && stdin) {\n            child.stdin.write(stdin);\n            child.stdin.end();\n        }\n\n        if (child.stdout) {\n            child.stdout.on(\"data\", (chunk: Buffer | string) => {\n                stdout += chunk.toString();\n            });\n        }\n\n        if (child.stderr) {\n            child.stderr.on(\"data\", (chunk: Buffer | string) => {\n                stderr += chunk.toString();\n            });\n        }\n\n        child.on(\"exit\", (code: number | null, signal: NodeJS.Signals | null) => {\n            // child process streams MAY still be open when EXIT is emitted (use close if need to ensure they're closed)\n            // \"close\" will come after \"exit\" once process is terminated + streams are closed\n            // so for now use \"close\" to determine if process was terminated too, that way you can access STDOUT/STDERR reliably for returning full output to agent\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"EXIT\", { code, signal });\n        });\n        child.on(\"spawn\", () => {\n            // emitted after child process starts successfully\n            // if child doesn't start, error emitted instead\n            // emitted BEFORE any data received via stdout/stderr\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"SPAWN\")\n        });\n\n        // Timeout handling – kill the whole process group after the supplied timeout.\n        let timer: NodeJS.Timeout | null = null;\n\n        let timeoutMs = Number(runProcessArgs?.timeout_ms);\n        if (Number.isNaN(timeoutMs)) {\n            timeoutMs = 30_000;\n        }\n        timer = setTimeout(() => {\n            if (process.platform !== \"win32\") {\n                if (child.pid) { try { process.kill(-child.pid, \"SIGTERM\"); } catch (_) {} }\n            } else {\n                child.kill(\"SIGTERM\");\n            }\n            const killTimeout = setTimeout(() => {\n                if (process.platform !== \"win32\") {\n                    if (child.pid) { try { process.kill(-child.pid, \"SIGKILL\"); } catch (_) {} }\n                } else {\n                    child.kill(\"SIGKILL\");\n                }\n            }, 2000);\n            const clearKill = () => clearTimeout(killTimeout);\n            child.once(\"exit\", clearKill);\n            child.once(\"close\", clearKill);\n        }, timeoutMs);\n\n        child.on(\"error\", (err: Error) => {\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"ERROR\");\n            // ChildProcess 'error' docs: https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#event-error\n            // error running process\n            // IIUC not just b/c of command failed w/ non-zero exit code\n            const result: SpawnFailure = {\n                stdout,\n                stderr,\n                //\n                // one of these will always be non-null\n                code: (err as any).code, // set if process exited, else null\n                signal: (err as any).signal, // set if process was terminated by signal, else null\n                //\n                message: err.message,\n                // ? killed: (err as any).killed\n            };\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"ERROR_RESULT\", result);\n            settle(result, true);\n        });\n\n        child.on(\"close\", (code: number | null, signal: NodeJS.Signals | null) => {\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"CLOSE\");\n            // ChildProcess 'close' docs: https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#event-close\n            //   'close' is after child process ends AND stdio streams are closed\n            //   - after 'exit' or 'error'\n\n            //   either code is set, or signal, but NOT BOTH\n            //   signal if process killed\n            // FYI close does not mean code==0\n            const result: SpawnResult = {\n                stdout,\n                stderr,\n                //\n                code: code ?? undefined,\n                signal: signal ?? undefined,\n                //\n            };\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"CLOSE_RESULT\", result);\n            settle(result, false);\n        });\n    });\n    // FYI later (when needed) I can map this onto the promise that comes back from runProcess too (and tie into that unit test I have that needs pid to terminate it)\n    // Resolve the underlying spawn result, then map to CallToolResult including PID.\n    promise.pid = child_pid;\n    return promise\n}","start_column_base0":7,"type":"ts","signature":"function spawn_wrapped(\n    command: string,\n    args: string[],\n    runProcessArgs: RunProcessArgs,\n): SpawnPromise {\n    const startTime = performance.now();\n\n    const options: ObjectEncodingOptions & SpawnOptions = {\n        // spawn options: https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#child_processspawncommand-args-options\n        encoding: \"utf8\"\n    };\n    if (runProcessArgs?.cwd) {\n        options.cwd = String(runProcessArgs.cwd);\n    }\n    const stdin = runProcessArgs?.stdin ? String(runProcessArgs.stdin) : undefined;\n\n    const logWithElapsedTime = (msg: string, ...rest: any[]) => {\n        if (!is_verbose) return;\n\n        const elapsed = ((performance.now() - startTime) / 1000).toFixed(3);\n        verbose_log(`[${elapsed}s] ${msg}`, ...rest, command, args);\n    };\n\n    let child_pid;\n    const promise: SpawnPromise = new Promise<CallToolResult>((resolve, reject) => {\n        if (!stdin) {\n            // PRN windowsHide on Windows, signal, killSignal\n            // FYI spawn_options.stdio => default is perfect ['pipe', 'pipe', 'pipe'] \n            //     order: [STDIN, STDOUT, STDERR]\n            //     https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#optionsstdio \n            //     'ignore' attaches /dev/null\n            //     do not set 'inherit' (causes ripgrep to see STDIN socket and search it, thus hanging)\n            options.stdio = ['ignore', 'pipe', 'pipe'];\n        }\n\n        // remove timeout on spawn options (if set) so the built‑in spawn timeout does not interfere\n        delete (options as any).timeout;\n\n        // Use a detached child so we can kill the entire process group.\n        options.detached = true;\n\n        let settled = false;\n        const settle = (result: SpawnResult, isError: boolean) => {\n            if (settled) return;\n            settled = true;\n            if (timer) clearTimeout(timer);\n            if (isError) {\n                resolve(resultFor(result));\n            } else {\n                resolve(resultFor(result));\n            }\n        };\n\n        const child = spawn(command, args, options);\n        logWithElapsedTime(`START SPAWN child.pid: ${child.pid}`);\n        child_pid = child.pid;\n\n        let stdout = \"\";\n        let stderr = \"\";\n\n        if (child.stdin && stdin) {\n            child.stdin.write(stdin);\n            child.stdin.end();\n        }\n\n        if (child.stdout) {\n            child.stdout.on(\"data\", (chunk: Buffer | string) => {\n                stdout += chunk.toString();\n            });\n        }\n\n        if (child.stderr) {\n            child.stderr.on(\"data\", (chunk: Buffer | string) => {\n                stderr += chunk.toString();\n            });\n        }\n\n        child.on(\"exit\", (code: number | null, signal: NodeJS.Signals | null) => {\n            // child process streams MAY still be open when EXIT is emitted (use close if need to ensure they're closed)\n            // \"close\" will come after \"exit\" once process is terminated + streams are closed\n            // so for now use \"close\" to determine if process was terminated too, that way you can access STDOUT/STDERR reliably for returning full output to agent\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"EXIT\", { code, signal });\n        });\n        child.on(\"spawn\", () => {\n            // emitted after child process starts successfully\n            // if child doesn't start, error emitted instead\n            // emitted BEFORE any data received via stdout/stderr\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"SPAWN\")\n        });\n\n        // Timeout handling – kill the whole process group after the supplied timeout.\n        let timer: NodeJS.Timeout | null = null;\n\n        let timeoutMs = Number(runProcessArgs?.timeout_ms);\n        if (Number.isNaN(timeoutMs)) {\n            timeoutMs = 30_000;\n        }\n        timer = setTimeout(() => {\n            if (process.platform !== \"win32\") {\n                if (child.pid) { try { process.kill(-child.pid, \"SIGTERM\"); } catch (_) {} }\n            } else {\n                child.kill(\"SIGTERM\");\n            }\n            const killTimeout = setTimeout(() => {\n                if (process.platform !== \"win32\") {\n                    if (child.pid) { try { process.kill(-child.pid, \"SIGKILL\"); } catch (_) {} }\n                } else {\n                    child.kill(\"SIGKILL\");\n                }\n            }, 2000);\n            const clearKill = () => clearTimeout(killTimeout);\n            child.once(\"exit\", clearKill);\n            child.once(\"close\", clearKill);\n        }, timeoutMs);\n\n        child.on(\"error\", (err: Error) => {\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"ERROR\");\n            // ChildProcess 'error' docs: https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#event-error\n            // error running process\n            // IIUC not just b/c of command failed w/ non-zero exit code\n            const result: SpawnFailure = {\n                stdout,\n                stderr,\n                //\n                // one of these will always be non-null\n                code: (err as any).code, // set if process exited, else null\n                signal: (err as any).signal, // set if process was terminated by signal, else null\n                //\n                message: err.message,\n                // ? killed: (err as any).killed\n            };\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"ERROR_RESULT\", result);\n            settle(result, true);\n        });\n\n        child.on(\"close\", (code: number | null, signal: NodeJS.Signals | null) => {\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"CLOSE\");\n            // ChildProcess 'close' docs: https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#event-close\n            //   'close' is after child process ends AND stdio streams are closed\n            //   - after 'exit' or 'error'\n\n            //   either code is set, or signal, but NOT BOTH\n            //   signal if process killed\n            // FYI close does not mean code==0\n            const result: SpawnResult = {\n                stdout,\n                stderr,\n                //\n                code: code ?? undefined,\n                signal: signal ?? undefined,\n                //\n            };\n            logWithElapsedTime(\"CLOSE_RESULT\", result);\n            settle(result, false);\n        });\n    });\n    // FYI later (when needed) I can map this onto the promise that comes back from runProcess too (and tie into that unit test I have that needs pid to terminate it)\n    // Resolve the underlying spawn result, then map to CallToolResult including PID.\n    promise.pid = child_pid;\n    return promise\n}","start_column_base0":7,"type":"ts","signature":"function });\n}","start_column_base0":0,"type":"ts","signature":"async function fishWorkaround(\n    interpreter: string,\n    stdin: string,\n    options: ObjectEncodingOptions & ExecOptions\n): messages.content:
You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers using code comments.
- NEVER change **unrelated code and comments.**
- Never add new comments to the end of a line.
- Never add trivial comments.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer readable code over of comments.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

For tool use, never modify files outside of the current working directory unless the user requested it:
~/repos/github/g0t4/mcp-server-commands

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`


## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Include "Co-Authored-By: gptoss120b"


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.


## Use `apply_patch` to edit files.

Your patch language is a stripped‑down, file‑oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply. You can think of it as a high‑level envelope:

*** Begin Patch
[ one or more file sections ]
*** End Patch

Within that envelope, you get a sequence of file operations.
You MUST include a header to specify the action you are taking.
Each operation starts with one of three headers:

*** Add File: <path> - create a new file. Every following line is a + line (the initial contents).
*** Delete File: <path> - remove an existing file. Nothing follows.
*** Update File: <path> - patch an existing file in place (optionally with a rename).

If you want to rename the file too, immediately follow with:
*** Move to: <new path>

Then one or more “hunks”, each introduced by @@ (optionally followed by a hunk header).
Within a hunk each line starts with:

- for inserted text,
* for removed text, or
  space ( ) for context.
  At the end of a truncated hunk you can emit *** End of File.

Here is the grammar:

Patch := Begin { FileOp } End
Begin := "*** Begin Patch" NEWLINE
End := "*** End Patch" NEWLINE
FileOp := AddFile | DeleteFile | UpdateFile
AddFile := "*** Add File: " path NEWLINE { "+" line NEWLINE }
DeleteFile := "*** Delete File: " path NEWLINE
UpdateFile := "*** Update File: " path NEWLINE [ MoveTo ] { Hunk }
MoveTo := "*** Move to: " newPath NEWLINE
Hunk := "@@" [ header ] NEWLINE { HunkLine } [ "*** End of File" NEWLINE ]
HunkLine := (" " | "-" | "+") text NEWLINE

A full patch can combine several operations:

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: hello.txt
+Hello world
*** Update File: src/app.py
*** Move to: src/main.py
@@ def greet():
-print("Hi")
+print("Hello, world!")
*** Delete File: obsolete.txt
*** End Patch

It is important to remember:

- You must include a header with your intended action (Add/Delete/Update)
- You must prefix new lines with `+` even when creating a new file


Tips on a failure:

- You only need enough context for a unique match!
- The line(s) you replace are context too; if they’re unique, no additional context is needed.
- Split bigger patches into smaller patches, i.e. if editing two functions, try each separately!
- Watch out for whitespace differences, i.e. blank lines

Please be curteous:

- NEVER leave comments about removed code, just get rid of it. This code lives in a git repo.
- Check your work! Especially after a failure.

### More Examples

Here's a patch file to change the `win` property's type:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@ @dataclass
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
-    gap_rms: TimeSeries
-    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
+    gap_rms: TimeSeries
+    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
*** End Patch
```

Which can be simplified to:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

Or, this could work if the `win` property is unique:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-    win: GapWindow
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

## General Code Preferences

- When rewriting code, leave unrelated code and unrelated comments as is.
- When an if statement has an AMBIGUOUS condition, extract a variable to meaningfully name it, for example:
```lua
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```

## TypeScript Code Preferences

- Use camelCase
- Use PascalCase for classes and interfaces
- Terminate with a semicolon.

argv.description: Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.

timeout_ms.description: Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms

command_line.description: Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.

stdin.description: Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.

function.name: run_process

patch.description: file changes in custom diff format

embed_top_k.description: number of embeddings matches to consider for reranking

query.description: query text, what you are looking for

top_k.description: number of matches to return (post reranking)

filetype.description: limit matches to a vim compatible filetype. default includes all filetypes

instruct.description: instructions for the query, explain the type of query

function.name: semantic_grep
function.description: Retrieval tool (the R in RAG) for code and documents in the current workspace. Uses a vector store with embeddings of the entire codebase. And a re-ranker for sorting results.
